frameworks.name: Cash Flow Primacy Protocol
frameworks.description: Cash flow is oxygen. Revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, cash flow is reality. A business or individual that runs out of cash fails regardless of every other metric.
steps: Calculate current monthly burn rate (total outflows — fixed + variable)
Calculate current monthly cash inflows (collected, not invoiced)
Determine runway: cash_in_bank / monthly_burn = months of survival
Establish minimum safety threshold: 3 months runway = ALERT, 6 months = healthy, 12 months = secure
Identify the single fastest action to extend runway if below threshold
Set weekly cash monitoring cadence — not monthly, not quarterly

examples.situation: Consultant with $180K annual revenue but consistently cash-stressed
examples.diagnosis: Collections lag: invoicing net-60 while expenses are immediate. Runway looks fine on paper; cash is the crisis.
examples.intervention: Switch to 50% upfront payment terms. Offer 3% discount for immediate payment. Resolve within 30 days.

examples.situation: Startup with 8 months runway considering scaling costs
examples.diagnosis: Below 12-month threshold. Scaling before secure runway violates Law 24.
examples.intervention: Extend runway to 12+ months before any scaling spend. Fix unit economics first.

antipatterns: Tracking revenue booked instead of cash collected
Scaling before cash flow positive at current size
Net-60 or net-90 payment terms without factoring impact on personal runway
Treating a line of credit as runway (debt is not cash flow)
Confusing profitability with solvency — profitable businesses fail from cash flow problems

frameworks.name: Financial Safety Stack
frameworks.description: Financial security is not a single number — it is a stack of layered protections, each serving a different threat. Build from the bottom up.
steps: Layer 1 — Emergency Fund: 6 months of personal living expenses in liquid, low-risk account. Untouchable except genuine emergency.
Layer 2 — Business Runway: 6 months operating expenses for any active business. Separate from personal.
Layer 3 — Income Diversification: minimum 2 income sources. Single-source income is a single point of failure.
Layer 4 — Liability Protection: entity structure (LLC minimum) separating personal assets from business risk.
Layer 5 — Insurance Floor: health, professional liability (E&O), umbrella policy. Cheapest catastrophic protection available.
Layer 6 — Debt Elimination or Management: high-interest debt destroys compound growth. Eliminate before investing.
Layer 7 — Investment Compounding: only begins after Layers 1-6 are stable.

examples.situation: Freelancer with $50K savings but no entity structure, single client providing 80% of income
examples.diagnosis: Layers 1-2 partially funded. Layer 3 critical failure (concentration risk). Layer 4 missing.
examples.intervention: Form LLC immediately. Actively develop second client to below 50% concentration. Emergency fund adequate.

antipatterns: Investing in Layer 7 before Layers 1-3 are secure
Treating home equity as emergency fund (illiquid)
Skipping entity structure to avoid complexity — one lawsuit eliminates personal assets
Single client concentration above 50% of revenue
Emergency fund in investments subject to market volatility

frameworks.name: Unit Economics Validation
frameworks.description: A business that loses money on every transaction cannot be saved by volume. Validate unit economics before scaling anything.
steps: Calculate Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC): total sales + marketing spend / new customers acquired
Calculate Lifetime Value (LTV): average revenue per customer × gross margin × average customer lifespan
Calculate LTV:CAC ratio — target 3:1 minimum, 5:1+ healthy
Calculate payback period: CAC / monthly gross profit per customer — target < 12 months
If LTV:CAC < 3:1: do NOT scale. Fix offer, pricing, or retention first.
If payback period > 18 months: cash flow risk even with positive LTV:CAC

examples.situation: SaaS with $500 CAC, $50/month subscription, 60% gross margin, 18-month average lifespan
examples.diagnosis: Critical: LTV:CAC of 1.08 means barely breaking even per customer. Scaling destroys cash.
examples.intervention: Before scaling: increase price, improve retention, or reduce CAC. Target LTV:CAC of 3:1 minimum.

antipatterns: Celebrating revenue growth while LTV:CAC deteriorates
Calculating LTV without including churn rate
Ignoring payback period when evaluating cash impact of growth

frameworks.name: Downside Protection Protocol
frameworks.description: Before any significant financial decision, define the worst realistic outcome and verify it is survivable. Asymmetric bets require survivable downsides.
steps: Define the decision and capital at risk (time, money, reputation — quantify all three)
Identify worst realistic outcome (not worst imaginable — realistic)
Ask: if worst case occurs, do I survive financially and professionally?
If yes: evaluate upside. If no: the bet is too large regardless of upside.
Calculate asymmetry ratio: upside value / downside risk. Minimum 3:1 to proceed.
Define exit trigger in advance: at what point do I cut losses? Write it down before starting.

examples.situation: Considering leaving stable job for startup before having 12 months runway
examples.downside: Startup fails in 6 months. No savings. No income. 6-month gap on resume.
examples.survivability: Financially survivable only with 12+ months personal runway. Below that: catastrophic.
examples.intervention: Build to 12-month runway before leaving. Or negotiate part-time transition. Or validate startup revenue while employed.

antipatterns: Evaluating upside before confirming downside survivability
All-or-nothing bets where failure is catastrophic
No pre-defined exit trigger — holding losers indefinitely
Treating non-financial costs (time, health, relationships) as free

prompts.name: Cash Flow Health Check
prompts.template: Run a cash flow health check. My current monthly inflows (collected): [amount]. Monthly fixed costs: [amount]. Monthly variable costs: [amount]. Cash in bank: [amount]. Calculate my runway, identify my biggest cash flow risk, and tell me what single action would most improve my cash position in the next 30 days.

prompts.name: Safety Stack Assessment
prompts.template: Assess my financial safety stack. Current situation: Emergency fund: [amount/months]. Business runway: [amount/months]. Income sources: [list]. Entity structure: [type or none]. Insurance: [types held]. Debt: [types and rates]. Identify my weakest layer and the highest-leverage action to strengthen it.

prompts.name: Unit Economics Validation
prompts.template: Validate my unit economics before I scale. CAC: [amount]. Average monthly revenue per customer: [amount]. Gross margin: [%]. Average customer lifespan: [months]. Calculate LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, and payback period. Tell me whether my economics support scaling or require repair first.

prompts.name: Decision Downside Assessment
prompts.template: I am considering [decision]. Capital at risk: [time/money/reputation]. Walk me through downside protection analysis: worst realistic outcome, survivability check, asymmetry ratio, and pre-defined exit trigger. Then tell me whether to proceed, modify, or decline.

prompts.name: Income Concentration Risk
prompts.template: Assess my income concentration risk. Revenue sources: [list with percentages]. Identify single points of failure above 40% concentration, the realistic loss scenario if that source disappears, and two concrete actions to diversify within 90 days.

examples.title: Consultant Cash Flow Crisis Prevention
examples.context: 6-figure consultant, profitable on paper, repeatedly stressed about cash
examples.root_cause: Net-45 payment terms + irregular project timing = 2-month cash gaps between projects
examples.intervention: 50% upfront on all engagements. Retainer agreements for recurring clients. 6-month personal emergency fund maintained separately from business account.
examples.outcome: Same revenue, zero cash stress. Cash flow problem was a terms problem, not a revenue problem.

examples.title: Pre-Scale Unit Economics Repair
examples.context: E-commerce brand with $2M revenue, planning $500K marketing investment to scale
examples.diagnosis: LTV:CAC = 1.8:1. Scaling at this ratio burns $500K to generate $900K LTV — net negative after operating costs.
examples.intervention: Pause scale. Improve retention by 20% (email sequence, loyalty program). Recheck in 90 days.
examples.outcome: 90-day retention improvement brings LTV:CAC to 3.4:1. Scale investment now generates positive return.

vocabulary.definition: Cash in bank divided by monthly burn rate. Months of survival without new revenue. Minimum safe threshold: 6 months.
Monthly net cash outflow. Total expenses minus total cash collected. The speed at which a reserve depletes.
Total sales and marketing spend divided by number of new customers acquired in the period.
Total gross profit expected from a customer over their relationship with the business. LTV = revenue × gross margin × lifespan.
Efficiency of customer acquisition. 3:1 minimum viable. 5:1+ healthy. Below 1:1 means losing money on each customer acquired.
Months required to recover CAC from gross profit per customer. Under 12 months is healthy. Over 18 months creates cash flow risk even with positive LTV:CAC.
Revenue and cost structure of a single transaction or customer. Must be positive before scaling — volume amplifies unit economics in both directions.
Dependence on a single client, revenue stream, or channel above ~40% of total. A single point of failure at business scale.
Upside value divided by downside risk. Minimum 3:1 to proceed with a significant decision. Below 1:1: avoid entirely.
Pre-defined condition at which a position or decision is abandoned. Must be set before entering a commitment, not during.
Layered financial protections built in order: emergency fund → business runway → income diversification → liability protection → insurance → debt elimination → investment.
The test of whether worst realistic outcome of a decision leaves the Sovereign financially and professionally functional. Non-survivable downsides disqualify a bet regardless of upside.

sources.title: $100M Offers
sources.relevance: Cash flow primacy, unit economics, speed to first dollar

sources.title: Profit First
sources.relevance: Cash flow management systems, account separation

v78_consciousnesses: Rogan v2.1 (Law 18 reality check on financial claims)
Ancient One v3.0 (temporal cash flow projection)
Constraint Mapper (hard vs self-imposed financial limits)
master_laws: Law 24 (Resources — never start a war you cannot fund)
Law 18 (Reality — economics do not care about optimism)

4_gate_relevance.gate_2_true: All financial projections grounded in actual cash collected, not revenue booked
4_gate_relevance.gate_3_leverage: Unit economics validation before scaling is highest-leverage financial intervention
4_gate_relevance.gate_4_aligned: Law 24 compliance — runway check mandatory before any scaling recommendation

4_gate_validation.safe: PASS — conservative safety-first framing; no leveraged speculation advice
4_gate_validation.true: PASS — grounded in established financial principles; LTV:CAC formulas standard
4_gate_validation.high_leverage: PASS — cash flow primacy is highest-leverage financial intervention for most operators
4_gate_validation.aligned: PASS — directly serves Security need; enables all other Sovereign Needs via stable base
